ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hot and sour soup; closest_parent 'soup food product' (FOODON_00002572) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Soups and broths) is the closest parent category; no specific hot and sour soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically represents hot and sour soup as a food entity; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: seasoned with vinegar and chili oil

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of hot and sour soup is approximately 300 g, typically served as a starter or light meal accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Sour Soup — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indian-Chinese hot and sour soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference entry for 'Soup, hot and sour, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 173568) and cross-checked against ICMR-NIN Table of Food Composition for comparable Indian-Chinese soup preparations. Per-100g values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation with tofu, mushrooms, egg, vinegar, soy sauce, and cornstarch thickening.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed soups and broth-based dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soup and tofu-based soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot and sour soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous broth-based soups with low starch content (vegetable soup GI ~30–40, University of Sydney GI Database). The cornstarch thickener contributes modest glycemic load; vinegar content is known to attenuate postprandial glucose response (Liljeberg & Bjorck, 1998, AJCN). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 4.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Hot and sour soup contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (960 mg per standard 300 g bowl), which represents 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request a reduced-sodium preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 4 per bowl, hot and sour soup is unlikely to cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; the acetic acid from vinegar may further attenuate glucose response, making this a relatively safe soup choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from chili oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium load from soy sauce and broth poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; saturated fat is low at 0.3 g per 100 g, but chili oil contributes unsaturated fats and the overall sodium content warrants caution for those on sodium-restricted cardiac diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and Sour Soup is a popular Indo-Chinese soup dish widely served in Indian-Chinese restaurants across India and Singapore. It is characterised by its dual flavour profile: heat from chili oil and white pepper, and sourness from rice vinegar or white vinegar. The soup base is typically a light chicken or vegetable broth seasoned with light soy sauce, thickened with cornstarch to a silky consistency, and finished with beaten egg ribbons stirred in at the end of cooking. Common solid ingredients include firm tofu, wood ear mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and sometimes shredded chicken or prawns. The texture is smooth and slightly viscous with tender vegetable and tofu pieces. Nutritionally, hot and sour soup is relatively low in calories (approximately 35 kcal per 100 g) and provides modest protein from tofu and egg. Its primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to soy sauce and broth; a standard 300 g restaurant bowl may contain close to 1000 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 35), partly because vinegar is known to blunt postprandial glucose response. This dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions but should be consumed cautiously by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium content. It is dairy-free and can be made halal. In Singapore, it is commonly found on the menus of Indian-Chinese restaurants and is served as a starter course.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indian-Chinese hot and sour soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from the closest USDA FoodData Central reference entry for Chinese restaurant hot and sour soup (FDC ID 173568) and cross-checked against ICMR-NIN comparable preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database broth-based soup entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA reference entry is a close compositional match and the estimation basis is clearly documented.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central reference entry for this soup category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the reference source for this preparation. Trans fat is 0.0, confirmed as negligible in broth-based soups per USDA data. The food is listed under Indian cuisine in the field survey source record, but the dish is more accurately classified as Indo-Chinese (Indian-Chinese fusion), a distinct culinary tradition originating from the Chinese immigrant community in Kolkata, India. The cuisine field has been updated to 'Indian-Chinese' to reflect this accurately. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement from the University of Sydney GI Database is not available for this specific dish. GL per serving = (35 × 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving / 100) / 100 = (35 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 4.0. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish conventions as this dish is served in Singapore; Chinese names reflect the standard Mandarin name for hot and sour soup (酸辣汤 / 酸辣湯).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. Identified that cuisine tag 'Indian' from field survey source was inaccurate; corrected to 'Indian-Chinese' to reflect Indo-Chinese culinary tradition.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list — all entries confirmed present verbatim. Verified GI category threshold: GI 35 ≤ 55 → 'low' confirmed correct. Verified -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are documented in notes. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings ('closest_parent', 'none'). Confirmed zero-macro justification not required as no core macro is zero. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass → PASS.
